[2] As first-line therapy combined with ipilimumab in metastatic or recurrent non–small cell lung cancer with a PD-L1 expression equal or higher to 1% with no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. [3] As first-line therapy in combination with ipilimumab, given with 2 cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy. Renal cell carcinoma After prior anti­angiogenic therapy. Previously untreated, of intermediate or poor risk, in combination with ipilimumab. [4] Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck Recurrent or metastatic with progressive disease on or following a platinum-based therapy. [5] Pleural Mesothelioma In combination with ipilimumab for unresectable pleural mesothelioma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of atezolizumab and bevacizumab plus chemotherapy (eg, paclitaxel and carboplatin) is the first-line treatment for adults with metastatic non-squamous NSCLC regardless of PD-L1 expression and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) or anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) genomic tumor alterations.

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications -- Extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide is the first-line treatment for adults with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer. [2] Non–small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC)

retrieved_docs.content: Small cell lung cancer Metastatic, with progression after platinum-based chemotherapy and at least 1 additional therapy. [6] Urothelial carcinoma Advanced or metastatic, showing disease progression during or after a platinum-based chemotherapy Progressive disease, within 12 months of neo or adjuvant treatment with platinum-based chemotherapy. [7] Colorectal cancer with microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) Indicated for patients 12 years and older with metastatic cancer following progression after treatment with fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and irinotecan. Nivolumab can be given as a monotherapy or in combination with ipilimumab. Hepatocellular carcinoma Following previous treatment with sorafenib, in combination with ipilimumab Classical Hodgkin lymphoma After relapse or progression following an autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) and brentuximab vedotin or at least 3 systemic therapies (including autologous HSCT)

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab monotherapy is administered as adjuvant therapy following resection and platinum-based chemotherapy for adults with stage II to IIIA NSCLC and PD-L1 expression on ≥1% of tumor cells (confirmed by an FDA-approved test).

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab monotherapy is indicated for treating adult patients with metastatic NSCLC who have disease progression during or despite receiving platinum-based chemotherapy. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy for NSCLC
retrieved_docs.content: Nivolumab is an IgG4 monoclonal antibody against PD-1. It is approved by the FDA for squamous and non-squamous NSCLC that has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy. It can be used in patients with high or low PD-L1 expression status. Pembrolizumab is also an IgG4 monoclonal antibody against PD-1. It is approved for pre-treated metastatic NSCLC with greater than 50% expression of PD-L1 and does not harbor EGFR and ALK mutations. It is also combined with pemetrexed and carboplatin for metastatic non-squamous NSCLC with less than 50% expression of PD-L1. Atezolizumab is an IgG1 antibody against PD-L1. It is approved for use in metastatic, progressive NSCLC during or following treatment with platinum-based chemotherapy. It can be used in patients who express EGFR and ALK mutations and fail targeted therapy. Bevacizumab is not considered immune therapy. It is an anti-angiogenesis antibody that inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A). It is primarily used in combination with platinum-based chemotherapy to treat non-squamous NSCLC. It is contraindicated in squamous cell NSCLC due to the risk of severe and often fatal hemoptysis. It is also used to treat breast, renal, colon, and brain cancers. [37] [13]

retrieved_docs.content: Nasopharyngeal cancer Mesothelioma Liver cancer Esophageal cancer Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma Colorectal cancer Breast cancer Prostate cancer Ovarian cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of atezolizumab and nanoparticle albumin-bound paclitaxel and carboplatin is the first-line treatment of adults with metastatic non-squamous NSCLC with no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations.

retrieved_docs.title: Pemetrexed -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant pleural mesothelioma [2] [5] [6] [7] First-line treatment for stage I to stage IIIA, regardless of histologic type (as monotherapy) First-line treatment for unresectable tumors, regardless of histologic type or patients who are not candidates for surgery (in combination with cisplatin) As an adjuvant medication Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), nonsquamous [8] [9] [10] [11] Locally advanced or metastatic, nonsquamous NSCLC (in combination with cisplatin) Metastatic, nonsquamous NSCLC in patients with no epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) or anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) genomic tumor aberrations (in combination with platinum chemotherapy and pembrolizumab) Maintenance treatment in patients whose disease has not progressed after 4 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy Patients with a history of prior chemotherapy (as monotherapy)

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal anti-programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) antibody that has been approved by the U.S. Food Drug Administration (FDA) for various neoplastic conditions as a single agent or in combination with other chemotherapeutic agents. These FDA-approved indications are discussed below. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab monotherapy is indicated for patients with metastatic NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression, regardless of histologic type. These patients are confirmed by an FDA-approved test and have no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. PD-L1 stained ≥50% of tumor cells (TC ≥50%) PD-L1 stained tumor-infiltrating immune cells (IC) covering ≥10% of the tumor area (IC ≥10%)

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: First-line single-agent use for nonresectable or metastatic NSCLC without EGFR/ALK tumor abnormality with tumor PD-L1 expression greater than 1%. In patients with previously untreated advanced NSCLC with a PD-1 score greater than 50%, first-line treatment of single-agent pembrolizumab showed a median overall survival of 30.0 months as compared to chemotherapy (investigator’s choice platinum-based regimen) control arm of 14.2 months(hazard ratio = 0.63), with a median follow up of 25.2 months. [6] Single-agent use for metastatic NSCLC with tumor PD-L1 expression greater than 1% who have progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy. (EGFR/ALK tumor abnormality patients with progression after targeted therapy for their respective tumor abnormality). In patients with previously treated, advanced NSCLC with a PD-1 score greater than 50%, subsequent treatment with pembrolizumab (10mg/kg) showed a median survival of 17.3 months as compared to the docetaxel arm of 8.2 months (p<0.0001). [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Indications -- Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic SCLC following platinum-based chemotherapy and at least one other therapy In patients with previously treated advanced small cell lung cancer, treatment with pembrolizumab resulted in an 18.7% objective response rate and a median overall survival of 9.1months. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1948
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, procarbazine, vincristine sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, ifosfamide, pembrolizumab, streptozocin, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, procarbazine), anthracycline chemotherapy (doxorubicin hydrochloride), vinca‑alkaloid compounds (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), a nitrosourea derivative (streptozocin), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate targeting CD30 (brentuximab vedotin), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor that blocks PD‑1 (pembrolizumab). The inclusion of both traditional cytotoxic chemotherapies and newer targeted or immunotherapeutic agents reflects the evolving multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to improve response rates while balancing toxicity. Effective patient care involves selecting appropriate agents based on disease stage, prior treatment exposure, and individual patient factors, often combining several of these medications within established chemotherapy regimens or as part of salvage and maintenance strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502

These drugs represent a diverse array of pharmacologic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, lomustin, mechlorethamin, streptozocin), a platinum compound (cisplatina), vinca alkaloids (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate), a bleomycin family agent (bleomycin sulfate), an anthracycline formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined or sequential use reflects the multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to eradicate malignant Reed‑Sternberg cells, achieve durable remission, and minimize disease‑related morbidity. Selection of a specific regimen is guided by disease stage, prior therapies, and individual patient tolerance, underscoring the importance of personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852

Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy agent that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, while its liposomal formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome) offers altered pharmacokinetics and reduced cardiotoxicity for various solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immunity in a wide range of cancers, including melanoma, non‑small cell lung cancer, and urothelial carcinoma. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) used to accelerate recovery of white blood cells after chemotherapy or bone‑marrow transplantation. Vinblastine sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát are vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, with vinblastine commonly used for Hodgkin lymphoma and testicular cancer, and vinorelbine

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, lomustine (lomustin), mechlorethamine, procarbazine, bleomycin sulfate, chlormethine hydrochloride, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, hydrochloride procarbazine, pembrolizumab, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين). These drugs encompass a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, procarbazine, chlormethine hydrochloride), antitumor antibiotics (bleomycin sulfate), nitrogen mustard derivatives (mechlorethamine), platinum‑free cytotoxic agents (lomustine), vinca alkaloids (vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), and immune checkpoint inhibition (pembrolizumab). Their inclusion in treatment protocols reflects the multimodal approach required for effective management of Hodgkin disease, combining cytotoxic chemotherapy with targeted immunotherapy to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1281

These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide), a bleomycin formulation, anthracyclines (doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal form), a nucleoside analog (streptozocin), a vinca‑alkaloid derivative (vinorelbine tartrate), a pyrimidine analog (chlormethin hydrochloride), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). The inclusion of both conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy agents and newer targeted immunotherapy reflects the multifaceted approach to managing Hodgkin disease, where combination regimens are often employed to improve response rates and reduce relapse. Recognizing the diverse pharmacologic options available for Hodgkin disease supports personalized treatment planning and informs clinicians about potential alternatives when standard therapies are contraindicated or ineffective.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851

Their combined inclusion reflects the multimodal approach often employed in managing Hodgkin disease, where chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy may be selected based on disease stage, patient characteristics, and prior treatment response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, ifosfamid, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbin tartarát are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease. The list includes classic cytotoxic chemotherapy drugs such as vincristine, bleomycin, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin (both conventional and liposomal formulations), ifosfamide, vinblastine, and vinorelbine, as well as the alkylating agent procarbazine (hydrochlorid prokarbazinu). In addition, newer therapeutic options are represented by pembrolizumab, an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor targeting PD‑1, and sargramostim, a granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor that can modulate immune responses. Together, these diverse pharmacologic classes provide multiple mechanisms of action for managing Hodgkin disease, ranging from direct tumor cell cytotoxicity to immune‑based strategies, underscoring the importance of a multimodal approach in patient care.
